slides.visual_description: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 1; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.
slides.narration_text: As you move from the crowded corridor to the dense EHR, The Training Gap, The Mindset Shift, and The Opportunity stop feeling like abstract labels. They name a daily clinical reality. We are trained to deliver care inside complex systems, but not always prepared to redesign the systems that keep producing friction. This module asks you to make the mindset shift from system employee to system designer. For clinicians closest to the workflow, that is where opportunity begins.

matched_visual_references.element: three bottom callouts: The Training Gap, The Mindset Shift, The Opportunity
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: The Training Gap, The Mindset Shift, and The Opportunity

slides.script_notes: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 1; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.

slides.visual_description: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 2; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: The phrase designer of it is the hinge in this definition. An innovation mindset means you do not have to accept today's workflow as the permanent shape of healthcare. You can notice where patients, teams, and information get stuck, then use your clinical judgment to imagine a better way forward. The point is not to leave evidence-based practice behind. It is to apply the same rigor and empathy to the system around care, not only to the patient in front of you.

matched_visual_references.element: centered definition statement about being a designer of the healthcare system
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: designer of it

slides.script_notes: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 2; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.

slides.visual_description: literal-visual slide generated by Gamma for card 3; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3 show that innovation leadership develops over time. In the first stage, you learn to recognize friction and turn it into a credible opportunity. In the second, you shape that opportunity into a solution that can be designed and pitched. In the third, the work shifts toward implementation and lasting change. What matters here is that this course starts with observation and framing. You are not being asked to fix everything today. You are learning how to see the problem clearly enough to begin.

matched_visual_references.element: three-stage roadmap labeled Sparking Innovation, Building the Solution, Driving Impact
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Stage 1, Stage 2, and Stage 3

slides.script_notes: literal-visual slide generated by Gamma for card 3; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.

slides.visual_description: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 4; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: From Define and Correlate to Create, the module lays out a progression from understanding to action. You will define the innovation mindset, analyze the trends that make physician leadership necessary, differentiate an idea from a defensible opportunity, evaluate failures through the M&M of Innovation, and create a personal baseline with an opportunity radar. That sequence matters because the course is building judgment step by step. By the end, you should be able to move from noticing a problem to working on it with structure.

matched_visual_references.element: stacked objective rows progressing from Define and Correlate to Create
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Define and Correlate to Create

slides.script_notes: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 4; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.

slides.visual_description: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 5; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.
slides.narration_text: Thomas Fogarty, Peter Pronovost, and Patricia Bath represent three different ways physicians have changed medicine. One advanced a device, one redesigned a safety process, and one pushed technology forward for patients who needed better options. The pattern underneath all three is the same. They stayed close to the care problem long enough to see what the system was missing. That matters for you because clinical proximity is not a limitation. It is often the advantage that lets a physician spot a solvable failure before anyone else does.

matched_visual_references.element: three innovator profiles: Thomas Fogarty, Peter Pronovost, Patricia Bath
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Thomas Fogarty, Peter Pronovost, and Patricia Bath

slides.script_notes: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 5; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.

slides.visual_description: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 6; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.
slides.narration_text: The phrase half-baked ideas have value captures why psychological safety is operational, not ornamental. Teams cannot improve a system if people are afraid to name what is unfinished, fragile, or clearly broken. Growth mindset matters here because early observations are rarely polished. They become useful only when people can say them out loud, test them, and learn together. If clinicians are expected to surface risk and redesign care, the environment has to make that honesty possible.

matched_visual_references.element: callout that half-baked ideas have value
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: half-baked ideas have value

slides.script_notes: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 6; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.

slides.visual_description: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 7; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: The Bloom's Taxonomy Level tells you this work goes beyond recall. In the M&M of Innovation, you are being asked to evaluate operational failure, trace it to root causes, and defend your conclusions in front of peers. That matters because innovation is not just brainstorming. It is a disciplined way of examining why a system keeps failing and what evidence supports a better design. The same rigor that makes morbidity and mortality review useful is what gives this assignment its value.

matched_visual_references.element: side-by-side objective statement and Bloom's Taxonomy Level panel
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Bloom's Taxonomy Level

slides.script_notes: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 7; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.

slides.visual_description: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 8; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: Personal Innovation Baseline and Opportunity Radar work as a pair. First, you take stock of the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats shaping your own clinical environment. Then you begin capturing recurring frictions that may deserve future project work. Together, those tools turn inspiration into a method. Instead of waiting for a perfect idea, you build a repeatable way to notice breakdowns, understand your leverage points, and decide which problems are worth pursuing.

matched_visual_references.element: split headings Personal Innovation Baseline and Opportunity Radar
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Personal Innovation Baseline and Opportunity Radar

slides.script_notes: literal-text slide generated by Gamma for card 8; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.

slides.visual_description: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 9; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.
slides.narration_text: Introduce Yourself, Name Your Bias or Assumption, and Reframe as an Opportunity turns reflection into a process you can use. Start with a recurring frustration from your own setting. Then surface the story or assumption that has kept that frustration in place. Finally, reframe it as a problem that can be examined and redesigned. That sequence matters because innovation usually begins before there is a solution. It begins when you make hidden assumptions visible enough to challenge them.

matched_visual_references.element: three numbered prompts: Introduce Yourself, Name Your Bias or Assumption, Reframe as an Opportunity
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: Introduce Yourself, Name Your Bias or Assumption, and Reframe as an Opportunity

slides.script_notes: Creative slide generated by Gamma for card 9; final visual grounding occurs in perception artifacts.

slides.visual_description: literal-visual slide generated by Gamma for card 10; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
slides.narration_text: The You Are Here marker at Module 1 situates this lesson inside the larger APC pathway. Foundations come first, then tools, then execution. That framing matters because this module is not trying to make you a finished innovation leader in one sitting. It is helping you build the habits of observation, framing, and opportunity recognition that later courses will deepen into design, pitching, and organizational viability. For now, the key point is simple. You are at the start of a real progression, and this first step teaches you how to see your clinical environment as something you can help redesign.

matched_visual_references.element: roadmap with You Are Here marker at Module 1 across three courses
matched_visual_references.narration_cue: You Are Here marker at Module 1

slides.script_notes: literal-visual slide generated by Gamma for card 10; source text constraints enforced for literal fidelity.
